flashcards.front: What is a structure in C?
flashcards.back: A structure is a user-defined data type that groups variables of different types under a single name. Each member has its own memory location. Declared using the 'struct' keyword.

flashcards.front: What is a union in C?
flashcards.back: A union is a user-defined data type similar to a structure, but all its members share the same memory location. Only one member can hold a value at any given time. The size of a union equals the size of its largest member.

flashcards.front: How do the dot (.) and arrow (->) operators differ?
flashcards.back: The dot operator (.) accesses members of a structure variable directly (e.g., student.name). The arrow operator (->) accesses members through a pointer to a structure (e.g., ptr->name), and is equivalent to (*ptr).name.

flashcards.front: How is memory allocated for a structure vs a union?
flashcards.back: A structure allocates separate memory for each member, so its size is at least the sum of all member sizes (plus possible padding). A union allocates memory equal to its largest member, and all members overlap in that shared space.

flashcards.front: What is typedef used for with structures?
flashcards.back: typedef creates an alias for a data type. With structures, it allows you to avoid writing 'struct' every time. Example: typedef struct { int x; int y; } Point; allows you to declare variables as Point p1; instead of struct Point p1;

A self-referential structure is a structure that contains a pointer to a variable of the same structure type. This is essential for building linked data structures like linked lists, trees, and graphs. Example: struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; };
Structure padding is the process of inserting unused bytes between structure members to align them to memory boundaries for faster access. This may increase the size of the structure.

flashcards.front: How do you pass structures to functions?
flashcards.back: Structures can be passed to functions by value (copy) or by reference (pointer). Passing by reference is more efficient for large structures, as it avoids copying overhead.
